critic_raw:
This experiment evaluates a CNN-RNN hybrid against logistic regression for 30-day readmission prediction.

The CNN-RNN architecture processes 'temporal sequences of treatments (procedures, medications)' while the logistic regression baseline receives 'all engineered features.' This claim of feature equivalence is not satisfied: the hybrid model ingests raw sequences (procedures over time), which the logistic regression cannot use. The engineered features given to logistic regression include derived summaries (medication complexity score, prior admission history), but the raw temporal ordering of procedures is only accessible to the hybrid model. This is a structural information asymmetry, not just an architectural difference. The claim that any performance gap is 'attributable to the hybrid model's architecture rather than tuning disparity' is wrong — it could equally be attributable to richer input information.

The department-level readmission baseline feature (population mean computed from training data) is included as a feature for both models. This is a form of target encoding: the feature is a direct function of the readmission label in the training population. This leaks label information into the feature space. If department assignment is correlated with temporal position in the dataset (e.g., one department expanded during the study period), this encoding could also introduce indirect temporal leakage between the training and test sets, since the encoding is computed on training records only but reflects the overall readmission rate of patients who were assigned to a department at any point during training.

The AUPRC is chosen as the primary metric. The paper states it is 'insensitive to class imbalance,' which is incorrect. AUPRC is actually more sensitive to class imbalance than AUROC because it depends directly on the positive rate. Its magnitude changes with the prevalence of the positive class, making comparison across datasets with different class frequencies unreliable. This does not invalidate using AUPRC as a metric, but the stated rationale is backwards and may indicate a misunderstanding of the metric's properties that could propagate into other design decisions.

The hybrid model tuning space includes learning rate (0.001, fixed) and specific architectural choices (2 CNN blocks, 1 LSTM layer) that are listed as fixed rather than searched. If the hybrid model's architecture is not part of the hyperparameter search, it may be pre-tuned to this dataset in ways not matched by the logistic regression grid search, creating an asymmetric optimization advantage.
all_issues_raised: The hybrid model receives raw temporal procedure sequences while logistic regression receives only engineered summaries. This is an information asymmetry, not just an architectural one — any performance difference could be driven by the richer raw input rather than the CNN-RNN architecture.

Department-level readmission baseline used as a feature is target encoding: it encodes the training population's mean label value and constitutes label leakage into the feature space, particularly if department assignment is correlated with temporal position.

AUPRC is claimed to be 'insensitive to class imbalance,' which is incorrect — AUPRC is actually more sensitive to prevalence than AUROC because its denominator depends on the proportion of positives. This suggests a potential misunderstanding of the primary metric.

The hybrid model's architecture (2 CNN blocks, 1 LSTM layer) is fixed and not part of the hyperparameter search. If these choices were previously tuned, the hybrid model has an optimization advantage not matched by the logistic regression grid search.
all_issues_adjudicated: The hybrid model receives raw temporal procedure sequences while logistic regression receives only engineered summaries. This is an information asymmetry, not just an architectural one — any performance difference could be driven by the richer raw input rather than the CNN-RNN architecture.

Department-level readmission baseline used as a feature is target encoding: it encodes the training population's mean label value and constitutes label leakage into the feature space, particularly if department assignment is correlated with temporal position.

AUPRC is claimed to be 'insensitive to class imbalance,' which is incorrect — AUPRC is actually more sensitive to prevalence than AUROC because its denominator depends on the proportion of positives. This suggests a potential misunderstanding of the primary metric.

The hybrid model's architecture (2 CNN blocks, 1 LSTM layer) is fixed and not part of the hyperparameter search. If these choices were previously tuned, the hybrid model has an optimization advantage not matched by the logistic regression grid search.
